region_note: This file is region-scoped. To change regions, update 'region', 'demographic_weight' values in 'classes', and all matrices to reflect the target country's class distribution and inter-religious composition.
model_note: Change 'model' to 'Neo-Marxist', 'SES Quintile', etc. for documentation purposes only. The engine ignores this field. To swap models entirely, replace the 'classes' array and all three matrices. Zero Python changes required.

classes.name: Elite
classes.demographic_weight_note: ~1% of India's population. Industrialists, top politicians, ultra-HNIs.
classes.economic_anxiety_multiplier_note: Scale [0.0-1.0]. Multiplied against the narrative's initial_arousal_spike for economic topics. 0.1 = Elites are barely emotionally reactive to economic shocks.
classes.base_literacy_range_note: [min, max] for COL_LITERACY uniform draw. Elites have consistently high media literacy.
classes.political_lean_bias_note: Added to U(-1, 1) political draw and clipped to [-1, 1]. Positive = skews Right. Elites strongly favor right-of-centre economic policy.

classes.name: Salariat
classes.demographic_weight_note: ~15% of India's population. Salaried government/corporate employees with job security.
classes.political_lean_bias_note: Mildly right-leaning. Favour stability and the status quo.

classes.name: Proficians
classes.demographic_weight_note: ~9% of India's population. High-skill freelancers, tech workers, consultants. High income but no job security.
classes.political_lean_bias_note: Slightly left-leaning on social issues, economically pragmatic.

classes.name: Old Working Class
classes.demographic_weight_note: ~30% of India's population. Factory workers, skilled tradespeople, unionized labour.
classes.political_lean_bias_note: Left-leaning on economic issues, susceptible to populist narratives about globalization and factory closures.

classes.name: Precariat
classes.demographic_weight_note: ~45% of India's population. Gig workers, daily wage labourers, domestic workers, informal economy. The largest class.
classes.economic_anxiety_multiplier_note: Near maximum. This class is extremely reactive to economic narratives (inflation, job loss, unemployment).
classes.base_literacy_range_note: Very low media literacy. Highly susceptible to misinformation and emotionally charged narratives.
classes.political_lean_bias_note: Strong left/populist lean. Frequently mobilized by religious-nationalist and populist rhetoric simultaneously.

friction_matrix_note: C×C matrix indexed by class id. friction_matrix[i][j] = sociological friction between class i and class j. RULES: (1) Must be square — C rows and C columns. (2) Diagonal must be 0.0 (no self-friction). (3) Should be symmetric. To add a new class: append a new row AND a new column to every existing row.
religion_conditional_matrix_note: C×R matrix. P(Religion | Class). Row index = class ID (0=Elite, 1=Salariat, 2=Proficians, 3=Old Working Class, 4=Precariat). Column index = religion ID — MUST match the ordering in religions.json (0=Hindu, 1=Muslim, 2=Christian, 3=Atheist). Each row must sum to 1.0 (auto-normalized with a warning if not). To add a new religion: append one column to EVERY row. To add a new class: append one new row. Pattern: lower classes are progressively more religious (Precariat is 99% religious, Elites are 40% Atheist).
